title: Rethinking Diffusion Model in High Dimension
abstract: Curse of Dimensionality is an unavoidable challenge in statistical probability models, yet diffusion models seem to overcome this limitation, achieving impressive results in high-dimensional data generation. Diffusion models assume that they can learn the statistical properties of the underlying probability distribution, enabling sampling from this distribution to generate realistic samples. But is this really how they work? To address this question, this paper conducts a detailed analysis of the objective function and inference methods of diffusion models, leading to several important conclusions that help answer the above question: 1) In high-dimensional sparse scenarios, the target of the objective function fitting degrades from a weighted sum of multiple samples to a single sample. 2) The mainstream inference methods can all be represented within a simple unified framework, without requiring statistical concepts such as Markov chain and SDE, while aligning with the degraded objective function. 3) Guided by this simple framework, more efficient inference methods can be discovered. Code is available at Supplementary Material.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
- This paper investigates the operational principles of high-dimensional diffusion models, particularly focusing on the impact of data sparsity on the objective function and inference methods. 
- It proposes a new perspective for understanding the training objective and introduces a unified inference framework called "Natural Inference," which aligns with this perspective and can represent various existing sampling methods. 
- The paper also explores how to design more efficient inference algorithms based on this framework.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
- Strengths
   - The paper reveals that in high-dimensional sparse settings, the diffusion objective degrades to predicting a single sample
   - It proposes a general inference framework—Natural Inference.
   - The framework enables the discovery of more efficient sampling methods and optimized coefficient matrices.
- Weaknesses
   - Much of the main body covers well-known concepts without offering clear explanations. For example, Equation 1 describes the forward process, while Equation 2 shows the reverse, but this is not clearly stated. The main contribution seems to be Section 4.2 on Natural Inference, but it lacks a strong connection to the earlier parts of the paper.
   - The introduction of Natural Inference in Section 4.2 is not very clear and feels abrupt. Earlier sections do not sufficiently analyze or build toward the core ideas suggested by the paper’s title. Many parts of the paper lack a strong connection to the  paper’s title and should more clearly align with the key points proposed in the title.

QUESTIONS:
- Could the authors clarify how the Natural Inference framework is theoretically grounded in the behavior of diffusion models under high-dimensional sampling settings?
- Furthermore, how do the contributions in Section 5 specifically support or relate to the central thesis of Diffusion Models in High Dimension?

LIMITATIONS:
- Please refer to the Weaknesses and Questions section.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The authors studied the understanding of diffusion model generation in high-dimensional problems. The authors studied a mixture presentation of the input data and observed the weighted-sum-degradation issue. The authors then presented a simple unified framework using the degraded objective function, which no longer requires statistical concepts such as Markov chain and SDE while aligning. The authors also studied more efficient inference methods.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
I only read the first half of the draft and did not proceed to the second part. The central results appear to be heavily motivated by Eq. (13); however, this equation raises some concerns:

1) For small or moderate values of t, the result holds but offers little insight; 2) $c_0$ in $p(x_t|x_0) \sim N(x_t; c_0 x_0, c_1^2)$ can be extremely close to 0. This leads to the mean $\mu$ becoming unbounded, which implies that the distribution places negligible mass on any particular value—essentially flattening the distribution and making meaningful inference difficult.

In addition, the draft is full of unsupported claims, e.g. 

for the claims such as "Traditionally, it is believed that in high-dimensional sparse scenarios, machine learning models cannot effectively learn complex hidden probability distributions,", ".. the weighted sum degrades to that single sample. This is more likely with sparse data." These arguments are pivotal, but no references are provided.

"When weighted sum degradation occurs, it is necessary to reconsider if diffusion models can truly learn the hidden probability distribution and how they work." need empirical support if some popular examples e.g. CIFAR10 generation has this pattern.

"Why can high order samplings speedup sampling ?" the question is well-understood and the answer is clear. 


minor: capitalize the letter for we in lines 104 and 89, this make in line 110

QUESTIONS:
"This make p(x0|xt) highly peaked at the closest X0, causing its mean to degrade from a weighted sum to that single sample." 

Q:

1. Why the mean matters here: for a symmetric mixture distribution around the origin, the mean is 0, but this doesn't imply any useful conclusion;

2. This is a standard phenomenon in high-dimensional probability. But why is it necessary is reduced to a single sample

LIMITATIONS:
NA
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper challenges conventional understandings of diffusion models in high-dimensional spaces by analyzing their objective functions and inference methods. The key discovery is that in sparse high-dimensional scenarios, the objective function degrades from a weighted sum of samples to a single sample, a phenomenon called "weighted sum degradation". The authors introduce the Natural Inference framework, which unifies mainstream inference methods (like DDPM, DDIM, and DPM-Solver) by treating them as linear combinations of predictions, guided by concepts like Self Guidance. This framework reveals that efficient inference methods can be designed by optimizing coefficient matrices, as demonstrated by improved FID scores on CIFAR10. The work reconceives diffusion models as fundamentally predicting original samples from noisy inputs, offering a simpler interpretation and practical guidelines for enhancing inference efficiency.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
The paper presents a thought-provoking re-evaluation of diffusion models in high-dimensional spaces, challenging the conventional understanding of how these models operate. The key insight that the objective function degrades to a single sample in sparse high-dimensional scenarios is both innovative and significant, as it provides a new lens through which to understand the success of diffusion models in such contexts.
The introduction of the Natural Inference framework is a notable contribution. By demonstrating that various inference methods, including DDPM, DDIM, and higher-order solvers, can be unified under this framework, the paper simplifies the understanding of these methods and highlights their underlying similarities. This unification also paves the way for the development of more efficient inference strategies.
The empirical results, particularly the analysis of weighted sum degradation in high-dimensional datasets like ImageNet, provide strong support for the theoretical claims. The demonstration that more efficient inference methods can be derived from the Natural Inference framework, as shown by the improved FID scores on CIFAR10, underscores the practical utility of the proposed approach.
Weaknesses:
While the experiments on ImageNet and CIFAR10 are informative, the scope of datasets could be expanded to include more diverse types of high-dimensional data, such as 3D molecular structures or text embeddings. This would help validate the generality of the findings across different domains.
The paper mentions computational efficiency gains but lacks a detailed quantitative analysis comparing the proposed optimized coefficient matrices with existing methods. Metrics such as inference time, memory usage, and FLOPs would provide a more concrete understanding of the practical benefits of the new approach.
While the concept of Self Guidance is introduced, a deeper mechanistic explanation of how different guidance types (Fore, Mid, Back) affect the generation process would be beneficial. Visualizations or case studies showing the impact of these guidance types on intermediate outputs could enhance understanding.

QUESTIONS:
1. How does the computational efficiency of the optimized coefficient matrix compare to the latest methods?

LIMITATIONS:
1. Only FID is used as an evaluation indicator, and other evaluation indicators can be considered.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper studies diffusion models in high-dimensional settings, arguing that they may fail to learn the true underlying data distribution and instead collapse into predicting individual training examples. The authors propose a unifying perspective on diffusion inference methods, framing samplers as linear combinations of past outputs. They further introduce an optimized sampling method and evaluate it empirically.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Weaknesses:

After reading the paper, I remain unclear about its core contributions. The central argument appears to be that for diffusion models (including score-based and flow-matching models), the key task is to predict $x\_0$ from $x\_t$, which has a Bayesian interpretation (as in the “guessing game” formulation [1]). However, this insight is already well-established in the diffusion community. The connections between score-based models and Markov Chain-based models (e.g., DDPM) have been thoroughly explored in prior work [2,3]. Thus, the observation that these models aim to learn conditional distributions is not novel.

The main conclusion of Section 3 also appears obvious. It hinges on the fact that if the data distribution is the empirical distribution, the score function becomes a Gaussian mixture, which is an observation well-covered in earlier work [4]. The so-called “weighted sum degradation” is an instance of the well-known curse of dimensionality. Line 112 suggests this degradation is what prevents the model from learning the true data distribution. However, this stems more from the assumption that the empirical distribution (a sum of delta functions) is the ground truth, rather than from the high-dimensionality per se. Even when the weights are not nearly one-hot (say in low dimensions), the model cannot learn the "true data distribution", since Eq. (15) and its corollaries are derived under the empirical data distribution assumption.

Section 4&5 proposes a generalization of ODE solvers for diffusion inference. However, it is unclear how this relates to the prior discussion of high-dimensional issues. The proposed method is also not adequately evaluated across diverse models and datasets, which limits the empirical support for the claimed benefits.

---

References:

[1] Kamb, Mason, and Surya Ganguli. "An analytic theory of creativity in convolutional diffusion models." ICML 2025.\
[2] Song, Yang, et al. "Score-based generative modeling through stochastic differential equations." arXiv preprint arXiv:2011.13456 (2020).\
[3] Gao, Ruiqi, et al. "Diffusion meets flow matching: Two sides of the same coin. 2024." URL https://diffusionflow. github. io (2024).\
[4] Gu, Xiangming, et al. "On memorization in diffusion models." arXiv preprint arXiv:2310.02664 (2023).

---

Minor:
- Many citations are incorrectly formatted. Use \citet or \citep appropriately.
- Lines 52, 54, and elsewhere: "Where" → "where", and punctuation should follow displayed equations.
- Line 60: Missing space after the word “Expectation.”
- The relation between “Markov Chain-based diffusion models” and “score-based diffusion models” is already well known. The paper spends nearly two pages re-explaining the equivalence among DDPM, SDE, and flow matching, which seems unnecessary.

QUESTIONS:
In Table 3, what is the NFE of all the compared methods? Is the proposed "optimized matrix" method using a same number of NFEs with the baseline methods such as DPMSolver? What is the order of DPMSolver algorithm used for comparison? Does the searched matrix generalize to other datasets?

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Reject

COMMENT:
This paper focuses on diffusion models in high dimensional settings. Through theoretical analysis, the paper shows that the objective function degrades into predicting individual training examples. The authors then propose a Natural Inference framework, which unifies mainstream inference methods.

The reviewers appreciated this study as timely and important, however, there are outstanding concerns after the rebuttal. First, the paper is not very clear in its contributions, especially on what the new insights are compared to existing literature and how the analysis before Section 4 motivates the natural inference framework. Second, empirical validations can be improved by incorporating more quantitative reports and larger as well as more diverse testbeds. The authors' responses partially addressed the first weakness. Therefore, to meet the acceptance threshold, a careful rewrite on the contributions in a convincing manner is needed. Stronger numerical demonstration would also strengthen the understanding of the paper.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
